title: Know Your Competition: Positioning When Every Street Has a Coffee Shop
subtitle: You do not need to beat your competitors. You need to be different from them.

toc.label: The Coffee Shop on Every Corner
Revenue Streams You Have Not Explored
Catering and Events: The Untapped Channel

sections.heading: The Coffee Shop on Every Corner
sections.body:
The specialty cafe market is growing, but so is the competition. In most urban areas, a customer has three or four options within a five-minute walk.

This is not a reason to panic. It is a reason to be clear about who you are and who you are for.

The cafes that struggle are not the ones with strong competitors nearby — they are the ones that try to be everything to everyone. They serve specialty coffee but also meal deals. They want to be a community hub but also a grab-and-go. They price premium but feel budget. The result: confused customers who cannot describe what makes the cafe special.

The cafes that thrive next to strong competition have a clear identity. They can finish the sentence: "We are the cafe that _____." We are the cafe that does the best brunch on this street. We are the cafe where freelancers work all day. We are the cafe with the best pastries in the neighbourhood. We are the cafe that knows every regular by name.

Clarity beats quality in a crowded market. A clearly positioned good cafe outperforms a vaguely positioned excellent one.

sections.heading: Mapping Your Local Landscape
sections.body:
Use T165-01 (Competitor Analysis Template) to build a clear picture of your competitive environment.

List every cafe, coffee shop, bakery, and food-to-go within a 5-minute walk (or your natural catchment area). For each one, assess:

| Factor | Your Cafe | Competitor A | Competitor B | Competitor C |
|--------|-----------|-------------|-------------|-------------|
| Coffee quality (1–5) | | | | |
| Food range (1–5) | | | | |
| Price point (€/€) | | | | |
| Ambiance (1–5) | | | | |
| Speed of service | | | | |
| Seating capacity | | | | |
| Google rating | | | | |
| Review count | | | | |
| Opening hours | | | | |
| Wifi / workspace | | | | |
| Loyalty programme | | | | |
| Delivery available | | | | |

Visit each competitor. Not to spy — to learn. Order a coffee, sit down, observe. What are they doing well that you could learn from? What are they doing badly that you can exploit?

Do this quarterly. Competitors change — new openings, closures, menu changes, refurbishments. Your positioning should respond to a live landscape, not a snapshot from two years ago.

sections.heading: Finding Your Gap
sections.body:
Your competitive advantage lives in the gap between what customers want and what competitors provide.

Common gaps in the cafe market:

**The workspace gap.** If nearby cafes have poor wifi, no power outlets, and uncomfortable seating for laptops, you can own the remote-worker segment by investing in good wifi, plenty of plug sockets, and table-height seating.

**The food gap.** Many specialty coffee shops have excellent coffee and mediocre food. If you can serve genuinely good food — fresh, well-prepared, interesting — you differentiate immediately.

**The hours gap.** If every cafe on the street closes at 4pm, opening until 7pm gives you a monopoly on the evening crowd. If nobody opens before 8am, an early opening captures the before-work market.

**The experience gap.** Chain cafes offer consistency but not personality. If you can create a genuine sense of welcome — remembering names, curating the space, hosting events — you have something Starbucks cannot replicate.

**The ethical gap.** Increasingly, customers choose based on values. If you can authentically demonstrate sustainability, ethical sourcing, community investment, and fair employment — and your competitors cannot — that is a meaningful differentiator.

**The price gap.** Not always about being cheaper. Sometimes the gap is that nobody on the street offers a genuine premium experience — and a segment of customers will pay more for better.

Pick one or two gaps. Build your identity around them. Communicate them clearly in your signage, menu, social media, and staff training.

sections.heading: Revenue Streams You Have Not Explored
sections.body:
Most cafes rely on one revenue stream: walk-in customers buying coffee and food. This creates concentration risk — if footfall drops (roadworks, weather, pandemic), revenue drops proportionally.

Diversified revenue is more resilient. Consider which of these you have not explored:

**Retail coffee beans.** If you serve great coffee, customers will buy beans to take home. Margin on retail bags is typically 50–60%. Use T169-01 (Retail Opportunity Assessment) to evaluate the opportunity.

**Merchandise.** Branded cups, tote bags, t-shirts. Not for every cafe, but if you have a strong brand identity, merchandise builds loyalty and generates margin.

**Catering.** Corporate meetings, events, office deliveries. See next section.

**Subscriptions.** Weekly coffee bag delivery, monthly unlimited filter coffee, pre-paid loyalty cards. Predictable recurring revenue is enormously valuable.

**Private hire.** Evening events, birthday parties, workshops. Your space is an asset that sits empty 12+ hours a day.

**Wholesale.** Selling your beans, cakes, or sauces to other businesses. Requires scale and logistics, but can be a significant revenue stream.

**Digital products.** Brewing guides, recipe books, online coffee courses. Low marginal cost once created.

You do not need all of these. But if 95% of your revenue comes from walk-in transactions, adding even one additional stream reduces risk and creates growth opportunity.

sections.heading: Catering and Events: The Untapped Channel
sections.body:
Catering is the most underexploited revenue stream in independent cafes. There are offices, co-working spaces, and meeting rooms within walking distance of most cafes — and they all need coffee and food.

Use T168-01 (Catering Menu Template) to build a simple catering offering:

**Start small.** A corporate coffee and pastry run for a meeting of 10 people. Filter coffee in a flask, a tray of pastries, some cups and napkins. Your cost: €15–20. Your charge: €50–80. The margin is exceptional.

**Build a menu.** Three tiers work well:
- **Basic:** Coffee and pastries (€5–7 per head)
- **Standard:** Coffee, pastries, and sandwiches (€10–15 per head)
- **Premium:** Full spread with drinks, food, and presentation (€20+ per head)

**Reach out.** Walk into local offices and introduce yourself. Leave a one-page catering menu. Connect on LinkedIn with local office managers. Most catering relationships start with a personal introduction, not an advertisement.

**Deliver reliability.** Corporate clients care about three things: on time, as ordered, easy to deal with. If you can nail these consistently, you will get repeat business and referrals.

**Scale gradually.** Start with one or two regular clients. Build the logistics (ordering, prep, delivery, invoicing) before taking on more. Overcommitting early and delivering badly is worse than not offering catering at all.

Catering revenue is particularly valuable because it is predictable (recurring orders), high-margin (bulk prep, no service overhead), and off-peak (prep during quiet hours, delivery before your own morning rush).

sections.heading: Benchmarking Against the Industry
sections.body:
Knowing your competitors is tactical. Benchmarking against industry standards is strategic.

Use T210-01 (Financial Benchmarking Dashboard) to compare your key metrics against independent cafe benchmarks:

| Metric | Industry Average | Good | Excellent |
|--------|-----------------|------|-----------|
| Gross margin | 65–70% | 72% | 75%+ |
| Food cost % | 30–35% | 28% | 25% |
| Coffee cost % | 20–25% | 18% | 15% |
| Labour cost % | 30–35% | 28% | 25% |
| Rent as % of revenue | 10–15% | 10% | 8% |
| ATV | €4.00–4.50 | €5.00 | €5.50+ |
| Revenue per sq ft/month | Varies | — | — |
| Staff turnover (annual) | 60–80% | 40% | 25% |
| Google rating | 4.2 | 4.5 | 4.7+ |

Benchmarks are guides, not targets. A cafe in central London has different economics to one in a small town. But if your labour cost is 40% when the benchmark is 30%, that is a signal worth investigating.

Benchmark quarterly. Track your own numbers against these standards and against your own trajectory. The most important comparison is with yourself six months ago — are you improving?

Join industry networks. The Specialty Coffee Association, local independent cafe groups, or even informal owner meet-ups provide benchmarking data and shared learning that no report can match. The cafe industry is surprisingly generous with knowledge — most owners are happy to share what works because they know a rising tide lifts all boats.
